title: Hierarchical Multi-agent Large Language Model Reasoning for Autonomous Functional Materials Discovery
abstract: Artificial intelligence is reshaping scientific exploration, but most methods automate procedural tasks without engaging in scientific reasoning, limiting autonomy in discovery. We introduce Materials Agents for Simulation and Theory in Electronic-structure Reasoning (MASTER), an active learning framework where large language models autonomously design, execute, and interpret atomistic simulations. In MASTER, a multimodal system translates natural language into density functional theory workflows, while higher-level reasoning agents guide discovery through a hierarchy of strategies, including a single agent baseline and three multi-agent approaches: peer review, triage-ranking, and triage-forms. Across two chemical applications, CO adsorption on Cu-surface transition metal (M) adatoms and on M-N-C catalysts, reasoning-driven exploration reduces required atomistic simulations by up to 90% relative to trial-and-error selection. Reasoning trajectories reveal chemically grounded decisions that cannot be explained by stochastic sampling or semantic bias. Altogether, multi-agent collaboration accelerates materials discovery and marks a new paradigm for autonomous scientific exploration.
ai_for_science_reason: The paper presents a novel AI framework using large language models and multi-agent systems for autonomous materials discovery, directly contributing to scientific exploration.
implementation_justification: The framework is well-defined and applied to real chemical applications.
review_justification: The paper provides a comprehensive review of the multi-agent approaches used.
benchmark_justification: The framework is benchmarked against trial-and-error methods, showing significant improvements.
hypothesis_generation_justification: The system generates hypotheses through reasoning-driven exploration.
scientific_discovery_justification: The AI system actively contributes to the discovery of new materials.
experiment_design_justification: The AI autonomously designs and executes atomistic simulations.
material_discovery_justification: The primary focus is on accelerating materials discovery using AI.
molecular_design_justification: The framework is applied to molecular systems, though not its primary focus.
drug_discovery_justification: The paper does not address drug discovery.
causal_discovery_justification: The reasoning agents infer causal relationships in material properties.
biomedical_discovery_justification: The paper does not focus on biomedical discovery.
idea_development_justification: The multi-agent system fosters new ideas in materials discovery.
paper_title: Hierarchical Multi-agent Large Language Model Reasoning for Autonomous Functional Materials Discovery

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to introduce the MASTER framework, which equips ensembles of large language models with structured, collective reasoning for guided materials discovery. The authors aim to develop a system where large language models autonomously design, execute, and interpret atomistic simulations to accelerate materials discovery.
objective.evidence: We introduce Materials Agents for Simulation and Theory in Electronic-structure Reasoning (MASTER), an active learning framework where large language models autonomously design, execute, and interpret atomistic simulations.

knowledge_gap.answer: The paper addresses the gap in autonomous scientific reasoning within materials discovery, where current methods lack the ability to adapt strategies based on outcomes and require human intervention for failed calculations.
knowledge_gap.evidence: Despite this potential, reasoning-driven autonomy remains challenging in materials science. Materials design is resource-intensive, requiring specialized expertise and substantial computational effort. Even with advances in automation, the process from concept to verified material can extend over years.

answer: Introduction of a multi-agent framework (MASTER) that integrates large language models for autonomous materials discovery.
Development of a multimodal system that translates natural language into density functional theory workflows.
Implementation of hierarchical multi-agent strategies for reasoning-driven exploration, reducing required simulations by up to 90%.
Separation of reasoning and simulation tasks to allow structured collaboration and adaptive exploration.
evidence: We introduce Materials Agents for Simulation and Theory in Electronic-structure Reasoning (MASTER), an active learning framework that equips ensembles of LLMs with structured, collective reasoning for guided materials discovery.
In MASTER, a multimodal system translates natural language into density functional theory workflows.

Across two chemical applications, CO adsorption on Cu-surface transition metal (M) adatoms and on M–N–C catalysts, reasoning-driven exploration reduces required atomistic simulations by up to 90% relative to trial-and-error selection.

By explicitly separating reasoning from simulation, the design agents focus on interpreting trends, weighing evidence, and planning experiments, while the DFT and reviewer agents handle numerical execution and verification, respectively.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- VASPilot (2025) VASPilot enables end-to-end execution of VASP workflows spanning structure preparation, job execution, error recovery, and postprocessing. (Methodological precursors)
- DREAMS (2025) DREAMS extends autonomy to networked simulation environments with automated convergence checks and recovery from failure. (Methodological precursors)
- LLMatDesign (2024) LLMatDesign introduced a self-reflective loop in which a LLM refined its proposals using a surrogate model pre-trained on density functional theory (DFT) data. (Methodological precursors)
inspirational_papers.evidence: Systems such as VASPilot, DynaMate, and ChemGraph, perform procedural automation quantum-mechanical simulations, with VASPilot enabling end-to-end execution of VASP workflows spanning structure preparation, job execution, error recovery, and postprocessing. Beyond single-job automation, DREAMS and MOFGen extend autonomy to networked simulation environments. Moreover, LLMatDesign introduced a self-reflective loop in which a LLM refined its proposals using a surrogate model pre-trained on density functional theory (DFT) data.

steps.step: Design agents formulate hypotheses and deliberate on which material to evaluate next.
steps.input: Natural language objectives and accumulated simulation history.
steps.evidence: The design layer comprises a team of LLM agents that use natural language to formulate hypotheses and deliberate, individually or collectively, on which material to evaluate next based on the accumulated simulation history.

steps.step: Simulation agents convert high-level objectives into validated DFT workflows.
steps.output: Validated DFT inputs and atomic geometries.
steps.evidence: The simulation layer provides a multimodal interface that converts high-level simulation objectives into validated DFT workflows.

steps.step: Reviewer agent evaluates computed quantities and decides if further exploration is required.
steps.input: Computed quantities such as adsorption energies.
steps.output: Decision on whether criteria have been met or further exploration is needed.
steps.evidence: The computed quantities, such as adsorption energies, are then returned to the review layer where a reviewer agent determines whether the specified criteria have been met or further exploration is required.

tools.name: VASP
tools.description: Used for performing DFT calculations for the transition metal-adatom case.
tools.evidence: All DFT calculations for the transition metal-adatom case were performed using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP, version 6.4.2).

tools.name: ASE
tools.description: Used for constructing atomic structures and generating DFT inputs.
tools.evidence: The CODEX agent receives rich contextual information, namely the user’s natural language query, detailed ASE usage patterns for surface science applications, and representative DFT workflows.

benchmark_datasets.name: Transition-metal adatoms on Cu(100)
benchmark_datasets.data_description: DFT-computed adsorption energies for transition-metal adatoms on a Cu(100) surface.
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used to test the MASTER framework's ability to identify targeted binding energies.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: These architectures are tested using CO binding energetics across two chemical domains: transition-metal (M) adatoms on a Cu(100) surface and M–N–C single-atom catalysts.

evaluation_metrics.name: Adsorption Energy
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the energy associated with CO adsorption on different materials.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate the effectiveness of the MASTER framework in identifying materials with desired adsorption properties.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: CO binding energy serves as a key descriptor of catalytic activity and selectivity, governing reaction pathways in CO oxidation, CO2 reduction, and the formation of C2 products via CO dimerization.

methods.name: Hypothesis or Idea Generation
methods.description: The system produces candidate hypotheses or new research ideas from prior knowledge or external input.
methods.evidence: The design layer comprises a team of LLM agents that use natural language to formulate hypotheses and deliberate, individually or collectively, on which material to evaluate next based on the accumulated simulation history.

methods.name: Experimental design generation
methods.description: The approach includes producing experimental protocols, configurations, or evaluation strategies.
methods.evidence: The simulation layer provides a multimodal interface that converts high-level simulation objectives into validated DFT workflows.

areas.name: Chemical Sciences
areas.description: The paper focuses on materials discovery and CO adsorption energetics, which are central to surface chemistry and catalysis.
areas.evidence: We apply MASTER to the problem of CO adsorption energetics, which has been widely studied in surface science and heterogeneous catalysis.

areas.name: Applied Sciences & Engineering
areas.description: The proposed framework aims to improve the efficiency of materials discovery processes, which is a key aspect of engineering.
areas.evidence: Altogether, multi-agent collaboration accelerates materials discovery and marks a new paradigm for autonomous scientific exploration.

performance_summary.summary: The MASTER framework reduces required atomistic simulations by up to 90% relative to trial-and-error selection.
performance_summary.evidence: Across two chemical applications, CO adsorption on Cu-surface transition metal (M) adatoms and on M–N–C catalysts, reasoning-driven exploration reduces required atomistic simulations by up to 90% relative to trial-and-error selection.

baselines.name: Trial-and-error selection
baselines.description: A traditional approach for selecting materials for simulations.
baselines.evidence: MASTER identifies targeted binding energies within only a few iterations, whereas trial-and-error selection requires an order of magnitude more trials.

benchmark_datasets.name: Transition-metal adatoms on Cu(100)
benchmark_datasets.data_description: DFT-computed adsorption energies for transition-metal adatoms on a Cu(100) surface.
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used to test the MASTER framework's ability to identify targeted binding energies.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: These architectures are tested using CO binding energetics across two chemical domains: transition-metal (M) adatoms on a Cu(100) surface and M–N–C single-atom catalysts.

evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the energy associated with CO adsorption on different materials.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate the effectiveness of the MASTER framework in identifying materials with desired adsorption properties.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: CO binding energy serves as a key descriptor of catalytic activity and selectivity, governing reaction pathways in CO oxidation, CO2 reduction, and the formation of C2 products via CO dimerization.

limitations.name: Limited Generalizability
limitations.description: The framework is demonstrated on specific chemical applications, which may not generalize to other domains without further adaptation.
limitations.evidence: In this context, MASTER must learn and generalize structure–property relationships directly from first-principles data and navigate complex search spaces without human guidance.

future_directions.name: Extend to Other Structure Families
future_directions.description: Expand the framework to include other structure families, such as metal centers embedded in a pre-established N-doped carbon host.
future_directions.evidence: MASTER can be extended to other structure families, e.g., metal centers embedded in a pre-established N-doped carbon host, by providing the corresponding structural priors in context.
